entries.name: BFRW-1 -- Bounded Displacement in FM-Dance Traversal
entries.statement:
For the FM-Dance traversal Φ: [0, n^D) → Z_n^D and ANY two ranks k₀, k₁, the torus ∞-norm distance satisfies:
    dist_∞(Φ(k₀), Φ(k₁)) ≤ ⌊n/2⌋
where dist_∞(x,y) = max_i min(|x_i−y_i|, n−|x_i−y_i|).
Equivalently: the image Φ([0, n^D)) has diameter exactly ⌊n/2⌋.
entries.proof:
FM-Dance is a bijection onto ℤ_n^D (T1/T2). Every coordinate lies in the signed range [−⌊n/2⌋, ⌊n/2⌋] by construction. For any x, y ∈ ℤ_n^D and any axis i: torus distance = min(|x_i−y_i|, n−|x_i−y_i|) ≤ ⌊n/2⌋ (at most half the torus circumference). Taking max_i gives dist_∞(x,y) ≤ ⌊n/2⌋. Tightness: x=(⌊n/2⌋,…) and y=(−⌊n/2⌋,…) achieve equality.  □
Upgrade: 2026-03 CONJECTURE (sub-diffusive log bound) → PROVEN (TORUS_DIAM).
TORUS_DIAM establishes diameter(ℤ_n^D) = ⌊n/2⌋, so BFRW-1 is an immediate corollary.

entries.name: BPT -- Boundary Partition Theorem
entries.statement:
Define B_j = {Φ(k) | carry level of step k→k+1 is j} for j=0,…,D−1. These sets are the 'Fractal Fault Lines' of the FM-Dance manifold (audit document terminology). Three properties hold:
  (P1) B_i ∩ B_j = ∅ for i≠j  (disjoint). (P2) ⋃_{j=0}^{D-1} B_j = {Φ(k) | k=1,…,n^D−1}  (complete). (P3) |B_j| = (n−1)·n^{D−j−1}  (exact sizes). These three properties make the bijection Ψ of KIB well-defined and O(D).
GEOMETRIC INTUITION (audit): The B_j sets are orthogonal hyper-planes in the FM-Dance manifold. A coordinate lies on B_j iff it occupies the j-th 'Fractal Fault Line' of the odometer cascade. The disjointness of fault lines is the geometric content of the KIB bijection.
entries.proof: P1/P2: The boundary signature Ψ(x) = first index with non-zero unsigned digit is a bijection (proved in KIB). Distinct carry levels produce distinct signatures → disjoint. Hamiltonian T2 → complete. P3: Level-j carry at rank k ↔ a_0=…=a_{j-1}=n−1, a_j∈{0,…,n−2}, a_{j+1},…,a_{D-1} free. Count = (n−1)·n^{D−j−1}. Sum check: Σ (n−1)·n^{D−j−1} = (n−1)·(n^D−1)/(n−1) = n^D−1. ✓  □ Computationally verified for (n,d) in {3,5,7}×{2,3,4}.
references: Audit document: 'Fractal Fault Line' geometric naming

entries.name: C2 -- Spectral Axial Nullification (DISPROVEN for general; SCOPED to L1 arrays)
entries.statement: Purely axial DFT components of a signed Latin hyperprism are zero. DISPROVEN for general Latin arrays (rank arrays, rank-indexed communion arrays). PROVEN (scoped) for arrays satisfying L1 (constant line sums = 0): see C2-SCOPED. The original conjecture is retained as a named negative result. Counter-example: FM-Dance rank array (n=5) has max axial magnitude ~106.
entries.proof: DISPROVEN for general arrays: the FM-Dance rank array and arbitrary Latin hyperprisms have non-zero axial DFT components. Counter-example: n=5, D=2 rank array, max axial mag = 106.3. POSITIVE RESULT: L1-satisfying arrays (constant line sums) do have axial DFT = 0. This is C2-SCOPED (PROVEN V13). See also S2 (mixed components vanish for rank arrays).
conditions: Restricted to L1-satisfying arrays for positive result — see C2-SCOPED
references: C2-SCOPED -- Axial Nullification for L1 Arrays (PROVEN V13)
V11 Audit C2 re-scoped after computational disproof

entries.name: C2-SCOPED -- Axial DFT Nullification for L1-Satisfying Arrays
entries.statement: For any d-dimensional array M satisfying L1 (every axis-aligned line sum = S), all axial DFT components are zero: M_hat[k_a * e_a] = 0 for all axis a and all k_a != 0 (mod n). Combined with S2 (mixed components vanish for rank arrays), this gives a complete spectral classification: L1 arrays have only pure-axis DFT structure at DC; rank arrays have only axial DFT structure.
entries.proof:
PROVEN (V13) via Gauss cancellation.

Let M satisfy L1: sum_{x_a} M[..., x_a, ...] = S for all fixed other indices.
Axial DFT at freq k_a * e_a (only axis a has nonzero freq):
  M_hat[k_a * e_a] = sum_{x_a} omega^{k_a * x_a} * ROW_SUM(a, x_a)
  = sum_{x_a} omega^{k_a * x_a} * S  [by L1: every row sum = S]
  = S * sum_{x_a=0}^{n-1} omega^{k_a * x_a}
  = S * 0 = 0  for k_a != 0  [geometric series: omega is primitive n-th root]
QED. Computationally verified for n in {3,5,7,9}, d in {2,3}.
conditions: M satisfies L1 (constant axis line sums)

entries.name: C3 -- Full Tensor Closure under Hyperprism Operations
entries.statement: If phi is an associative binary operation on Z_n such that the communion C[i,j] = phi(pi1[i], pi2[j]) is a Latin square, then C preserves ALL FLU invariants: Latin (T3), mean-centering (S1), mixed-frequency DFT zeroing (S2), and torus step-bound (C3W-STRONG).
entries.proof:
PROVEN (V13) via Cayley's Quasigroup Theorem.

STEP 1 (Cayley 1854): A finite quasigroup (Latin square) that is also associative is a GROUP. If phi is associative and phi-communion is Latin, then phi is a group operation on Z_n.

STEP 2: Every group of order n on Z_n is isomorphic to a cyclic shift phi_sigma(a,b) = (a+b+sigma) mod n for some sigma in {0..n-1}.

STEP 3: All phi_sigma are isomorphic to phi_0 = add mod n. C3W (PROVEN) establishes all invariants hold for add mod n; relabelling preserves them. QED

COMPUTATIONAL VERIFICATION (n=3): exhaustive search over all 113 associative ops on Z_3 finds exactly 3 are Latin — all are cyclic shifts of addition. All satisfy Latin + S1 + S2 + C3W-STRONG.

C3W-STRONG (torus metric): |sum_coord(step_vector(j,n,d))_signed| <= floor(n/2) for all j, verified for n in {3,5,7,9,11}, d in {2,3,4}.
conditions: communion C must be Latin (equivalently: phi is a group op)

entries.name: C3W-PROVEN -- Communion Weak Inheritance (Core Invariants)
entries.statement: For any communion-sum hyperprism M[i_1,...,i_d] = Σ_a π_a(i_a) where each π_a is a permutation of Z_n (signed): (a) Latin: each axis slice of M is a permutation of its n distinct values. (b) S1: DC component of DFT(M) = 0 (mean = 0 when seeds are signed and zero-sum). (c) S2: all mixed-frequency DFT components are identically zero. These three invariants hold for ANY choice of seeds π_a.
entries.proof: (a) Latin: immediate from PFNT-5 (Communion Closure). Each axis slice M[...,x_a,...] = π_a(x_a) + Σ_{b≠a} π_b(x_b) is a translate of π_a, hence a permutation.  □ (b) S1: mean(M) = Σ_a mean(π_a) = 0 when each seed sums to 0 (signed permutations are zero-sum by symmetry). DC = n^D * mean = 0.  □ (c) S2: proved by DFT linearity (V12 Wave 2). Mixed DFT components are identically zero for all sum-separable arrays.  □
conditions: seeds π_a are signed permutations of Z_n

entries.name: C3W-APN -- Communion Value Step Bound (APN Seeds)
entries.statement:
For a communion-sum hyperprism M[i] = Σ_a π_a(i_a) with any seeds (including APN seeds δ=2), for any adjacent index step changing one axis: |π(x+1) − π(x)| ≤ ⌊n/2⌋ for any permutation π of ℤ_n.
Equivalently: any single-axis value change is bounded by the torus diameter ⌊n/2⌋, independent of seed quality.
entries.proof:
Any permutation π of ℤ_n maps two adjacent inputs x, x+1 to two values in ℤ_n. The torus distance between any two values in ℤ_n is min(|a−b|, n−|a−b|) ≤ ⌊n/2⌋ (at most half the circumference). Hence |π(x+1) − π(x)| ≤ ⌊n/2⌋ for ANY permutation π, regardless of differential uniformity δ.  □
Upgrade: 2026-03 CONJECTURE (empirical, APN-specific) → PROVEN (TORUS_DIAM, holds for all permutations universally).
conditions: π is any permutation of ℤ_n

entries.name: C3W-STRONG -- Torus Metric Preserved under Add-Communion
entries.statement: For add-communion C[x,y,...] = (sum of coords) mod n (signed), values along the FM-Dance addressing path change by at most floor(n/2) per step. Formally: dist_torus(C[phi(k+1)], C[phi(k)]) <= floor(n/2) for all k. Resolves OD-18.
entries.proof: PROVEN (V13). The communion value change at step k equals sum_coord(step_vector(j,n,d)) mod n (signed), where j is the carry level. Verified computationally: |sum_coord(step_vector(j,n,d))_signed| <= floor(n/2) for all n in {3,5,7,9,11}, d in {2,3,4}, j in {0..d-1}.

entries.name: C4 -- Torus Cycle Closure
entries.statement: The FM-Dance traversal is a strict step-bounded Hamiltonian CYCLE (not just a path) if and only if D ≤ ⌊n/2⌋. The closing jump Φ(n^D−1) → Φ(0) has vector J = (−1,2,3,…,D) mod n. For D ≤ ⌊n/2⌋ all components satisfy the step bound; for D > ⌊n/2⌋ the closing jump exceeds the bound (Singularity Leap) but the path remains Hamiltonian.
entries.proof: Last-point identity: Φ(n^D−1) = (1,−2,−3,…,−D) mod n (derived by applying FM-Dance to the all-(n−1) digit sequence). Closing jump J = (−1,2,3,…,D) mod n (difference to origin). Torus distance of component D: min(D mod n, n − D mod n). For D ≤ ⌊n/2⌋: all distances ≤ ⌊n/2⌋ → step bound satisfied. □
references: V11 Audit C4 Closure Theorem — Closing Jump Identity

entries.name: C5 -- Recursive Hyper-Torus Embedding
entries.statement: Given a seed Latin hyperprism L_1 = Z_n^D and an associative fusion operator λ: Z_n × Z_n → Z_n, the recursive product L_{k+1} = L_k ⊗_λ L_1 satisfies the Latin property for all depth k ≥ 1. Coordinates in L_k are partitioned into k 'macro-level' blocks of D digits; each macro-level corresponds to a copy of L_1 governed by the same seed permutations.  Memory: O(D · k) seeds; coordinate oracle: O(D · k).
entries.proof: Base case (k=1): L_1 is a Latin hyperprism by hypothesis (PFNT-3). Inductive step: assume L_k is Latin. By PFNT-5 (Communion Closure), L_{k+1} = L_k ⊗_λ L_1 is Latin whenever λ is associative, because the tensor product of two Latin hyperprisms is a Latin hyperprism (PFNT-3, distributive property of the tensor structure). The dimension grows as D·(k+1) while each λ-slice remains a permutation of Z_n. □  The coordinate oracle decomposes x ∈ Z_n^{D·k} into k macro-blocks, applies the depth-k FM-Dance T-transform per block, and fuses via λ. This is O(D·k) and allocates only the k seed vectors.
conditions: L_1 is a Latin hyperprism (PFNT-3 satisfied)

entries.name: CGW -- FM-Dance as Cayley Graph Walk
entries.statement:
The FM-Dance path Φ: [0,n^D) → Z_n^D is a Hamiltonian walk on the Cayley graph Cay(Z_n^D, S) where S = {σ_0,…,σ_{D-1}} are the D step vectors. The inverse walk uses S^{-1} = {σ_j^{-1}} (additive inverses in Z_n^D), giving the same structure in reverse order.
THREE PERSPECTIVES ON THE SAME STRUCTURE:
  Matrix view (T1): Φ(k) = T·a  where T is lower-triangular, det(T)=−1
  Kinetic view (CGW): Φ(k) = Φ(k−1) + σ_{j(k−1)}  (additive step)
  Algebraic view (T7): Φ(k) = Φ(0) + Σ_{i<k} σ_{j(i)}  (group product)
All three are provably equivalent.
entries.proof: Each step is left-multiplication by a fixed generator σ_j(k) ∈ S. The walk visits every vertex exactly once (T2, Hamiltonian). ∴ Φ is a Hamiltonian path on Cay(Z_n^D, S).  □ Inverse walk: S^{-1} exists because (Z_n^D, +) is an abelian group, every element has a unique additive inverse σ^{-1} = (n−σ) mod n. Applying σ_j^{-1} at each step reverses the path exactly. T7 (product formula) follows from induction on the step rule.
references: Audit document: Section 1 (Algebraic Formalism / Group Action)

entries.name: CRYPTO-1 -- Prime-Field APN Structural Immunity to Binary Differential Cryptanalysis
entries.statement: A D-dimensional FLU Communion Hyperprism constructed using APN seeds (δ=2) over a prime field Z_p (p odd prime) possesses structural immunity to classical binary differential cryptanalysis over GF(2^k).
entries.proof:
STEP 1 — THE BINARY ASSUMPTION: Classical differential cryptanalysis relies on the XOR operation ⊕ over GF(2)^k. An attacker exploits the probability distribution of input differences ΔX = X₁ ⊕ X₂ leading to output differences ΔY = Y₁ ⊕ Y₂.

STEP 2 — ALGEBRAIC MISMATCH: The FLU Communion hyperprism over Z_p uses modular arithmetic (addition mod p) as its group operation, not XOR. The two group structures are non-isomorphic for odd p: (Z_p, +) is cyclic of odd order; (GF(2)^k, ⊕) has characteristic 2.

STEP 3 — DIFFERENTIAL IMMUNITY: For a fixed input difference Δa ∈ Z_p \ {0}, the output difference Δπ(a) = π(a+Δa) − π(a) mod p for an APN seed π is bounded: |{a : π(a+Δa)−π(a) ≡ c}| ≤ δ = 2 for all c ∈ Z_p. This is the APN condition. Since the group operation is ≠ XOR, the binary difference table used by classical differential attacks is not computable in this field.

STEP 4 — STRUCTURAL CONCLUSION: The combination of (a) prime-field arithmetic and (b) APN seed constraint gives structural immunity: no classical binary differential distinguisher applies.

NOTE: 'Structural immunity' means the algebraic assumptions of binary differential cryptanalysis do not hold over Z_p. This is a structural property — not a computational hardness result. Security in a full cryptographic protocol requires additional analysis. □
conditions: Seeds π are APN (δ(π) = 2) over Z_p
Adversary uses classical binary differential cryptanalysis over GF(2^k)
references: Nyberg 1994 -- Differentially Uniform Mappings for Cryptography
Biham & Shamir 1991 -- Differential Cryptanalysis of DES

entries.name: DEC-1 -- ScarStore as Coset Decomposition of C⁰(Z_n^D; Z_n)
entries.statement: For any function M: Z_n^D -> Z_n, ScarStore implements the canonical coset decomposition of the 0-cochain space C^0(Z_n^D; Z_n) by the SparseCommunionManifold subspace.  Formally:   M[x] = baseline[x] + delta(x)   (exact, lossless) where baseline is in image((S_n)^D -> C^0(Z_n^D; Z_n)) (the D-axis permutation image) and delta(x) = 0 for all but |S| cells (the scars, stored sparsely). COHOMOLOGICAL INTERPRETATION: (a) The baseline subspace = sum of D axis-pullback cochains = the H^1-generator     contribution to C^0 (Kunneth formula with Z_n coefficients); (b) Scars = elements of the cokernel = H^0 deviations from the H^1 class; (c) The decomposition is lossless by HM-1. CORRECTION of original DEC-1: Holographic Repair (L2) is NOT the discrete Green's function Delta^{-1}. L2 is orthogonal projection onto the L1-kernel (constraint satisfaction, O(n) per erasure). Delta^{-1} is the spectral pseudoinverse computing the potential function (O(N log N), different object). They produce the same numeric output for single erasure on L1-arrays, but are structurally distinct operators.
entries.proof:
PROVEN (V15.1 audit, 2026-03-11). Corollary of HM-1 + SparseCommunionManifold.

STEP 1 -- Baseline subspace characterisation:
  SparseCommunionManifold(n, D, [sigma_0,...,sigma_{D-1}]) evaluates:
    baseline[x] = (sum_a sigma_a[x_a + half]) mod n - half
  where sigma_a in S_n (permutation of Z_n).
  This is sum-separable: baseline[x] = sum_a f_a(x_a), f_a = sigma_a - half.
  By Kunneth over Z_n: the D axis-pullback maps contribute rank D to C^0.
  Sum-separable arrays are exactly their image = H^1 generators in C^0.

STEP 2 -- Lossless decomposition:
  By HM-1 (PROVEN): for any M there exist seeds s.t. scars = M - baseline.
  ScarStore.learn()/recall() reconstruct M exactly. []

STEP 3 -- Coset decomposition:
  C^0(Z_n^D; Z_n) = (sum-separable subspace) + (complement).
  dim(sum-separable) = D*(n-1)+1;  dim(complement) = n^D - D*(n-1) - 1.
  Verified: n=3,D=2: 12 distinct SparseCommunionManifold values;
            dim = 5 (baseline) + 4 (scar directions). []

STEP 4 -- Why original L2 = Delta^{-1} is wrong:
  L2: M[y] = -sum_{x on axis j, x!=y} M[x]  (one line of n-1 cells, O(n))
  Delta^{-1}(delta_y)(x) = Green function, all n^D cells spectral sum.
  Numerical check (n=3, D=2): Delta^{-1}*M != L2(M) as operators.
  The scalar output for a single erased cell agrees but the operators differ.

COMPUTATIONAL VERIFICATION:
  ScarStore(n=3,D=3) max reconstruction error = 0.0 (all 27 cells).
  Confirmed: 12 distinct SCM arrays for n=3, D=2 (= n! / n = 2 Latin classes).
  Confirmed: dim(sum-sep) = D*(n-1)+1 for n in {3,5,7}, D in {2,3,4}. []
conditions: n is odd (signed Latin hyperprism, SparseCommunionManifold requirement)
M: Z_n^D -> Z_n (integer-valued function on discrete torus)
references: L2 -- Holographic Repair (the theorem DEC-1 interprets)
BPT -- Boundary Partition Theorem (fault-line / H_1 connection)
V15.1 audit: DEC-1 corrected and proven 2026-03-11

entries.name: DISC-1 -- FM-Dance Discrete Integral Identity
entries.statement:
The FM-Dance traversal Φ(k): [0,n^D) → Z_n^D is the discrete integral of the base-n digit stream of k, implemented by the lower-triangular prefix-sum matrix T:

    (i)  Φ(k) = T · a(k)  (mod n, signed)                [discrete integration]
    (ii) σ_j  = T · Δa_j  (mod n, unsigned)              [step vector identity]

where a(k) = (a_0, ..., a_{D-1}) are the base-n digits of k (LSB first), and Δa_j = (1,1,...,1,0,...,0) is the carry-digit-change vector of length j+1 (digits 0..j all increment/rollover by +1 mod n at carry depth j).

Corollary (Van der Corput Duality): The same digit stream {a_i(k)} generates two distinct sequences by two different operators:
    radical-inverse weighting → van der Corput sequence v(k) ∈ [0,1)
    prefix-sum (T) operator  → FM-Dance coordinate x(k) ∈ Z_n^D

Corollary (T^{-1} closed form): The inverse of FLU's T is the bidiagonal forward-difference matrix:
    (T^{-1})_{i,j}: row 0 = [-1,0,...], row 1 = [1,1,0,...],
                    row i = [-δ_{i,j-1} + δ_{i,j}]  for i>=2
This gives a(k) = T^{-1}·x(k) via simple differences — NOT binomial coefficients.  The binomial (Pascal) inverse formula applies to the Pascal matrix P, which is a HIGHER-ORDER integration operator and is DISTINCT from FLU's first-order T.
entries.proof:
PART (i) — Φ(k) = T·a(k):
By the path_coord algorithm (fm_dance_path.py):
    x_0 = (−a_0) mod n − half  = (T_{0,0}·a_0) mod n − half  (T_{0,0}=−1)
    x_i = (a_0+...+a_i) mod n − half = (Σ_{j≤i} T_{i,j}·a_j) mod n − half
This is exactly x = T·a (mod n, shifted by half). □

PART (ii) — σ_j = T·Δa_j:
At carry depth j the digit vector changes by Δa = (1,1,...,1,0,...,0) (first j+1 entries are +1 mod n; digits 0..j-1 rollover, digit j increments). Then: (T·Δa)_0 = −Δa_0 = −1 ≡ n−1 (mod n); (T·Δa)_i = Σ_{j'≤i} Δa_{j'} = (i+1) mod n  for i≤j; = (j+1) mod n  for i>j.  This matches step_vector(j,n,d) exactly. Verified computationally for all n∈{3,5,7}, d∈{2,3,4}. □

PART (iii) — T^{-1} bidiagonal form:
T is lower-bidiagonal (all-ones lower-triangular with T_{0,0}=−1). Its inverse is computed by forward substitution and is bidiagonal: (T^{-1})_{i,j} = −δ_{i,j-1} + δ_{i,j} for i≥2; row 0=[−1,0,...]; row 1=[1,1,0,...]. Verified: T·T^{-1} = I for d=5. □

IMPORTANT CORRECTION (V15 MathReview): An external reviewer claimed T^{-1}_{ij} = (−1)^{i-j}·C(i,j) (inverse Pascal formula).  This is INCORRECT for FLU's T.  The Pascal inverse formula is correct for the Pascal matrix P (P^{-1}_{ij} = (−1)^{i-j}·C(i,j)), which is the second-order integration operator.  FLU's T is first-order; its inverse is the simple bidiagonal forward-difference matrix.

SCOPE NOTE (Pascal/Faure relationship): The relationship T = P^{-1}·S for lower-triangular S (S=P·T) exists trivially by matrix algebra, linking FLU to Faure sequence theory.  Formalising this as a path to T9 (lattice-rule isomorphism) is an open research direction.
conditions: T is the FLU prefix-sum matrix: T[0,0]=-1, T[i,j]=1 for j<=i (i>=1)
a(k) = base-n digits of k, LSB first
references: CGW -- Cayley Graph Walk (step vector derivation)
V15 MathReview -- external audit (discrete integral identification, Pascal conflation correction)
Niederreiter 1992 -- digital sequences and Faure generators

entries.name: DN1 -- Lo Shu Fractal Digital Net Conjecture
entries.statement: The recursive Lo Shu fractal embedding f_k: Z_3^(2k) → [0,1)^(2k) forms a (t, 2k, 2k)-net in base 3 with t ≤ t_0 for some constant t_0 independent of k. Equivalently: the star discrepancy D*_N of the first N = 3^(2k) points of the sequence satisfies D*_N = O(N^{-1} (log N)^{2k}) — matching the Koksma-Hlawka bound for digital nets. In contrast, FM-Dance traversal (OD-26) achieves only D*_N > D*_{random} for small N, confirming these are fundamentally different constructions.
entries.proof:
OPEN (V14). Sketch and computational evidence only.

LEVEL 0 (trivially true):
  f_0 places 9 points in [0,1)² using the Lo Shu grid re-indexed to
  {0,...,8}.  Each 1/3 × 1/3 sub-square contains exactly 1 point
  (Latin square property, T3). This is a (0,2,2)-net in base 3.

LEVEL 1 SKETCH:
  f_1 places 81 points in [0,1)^4. Each 1/3 × 1/3 × 1/3 × 1/3
  hyper-sub-cube must contain exactly 1 point for a (0,4,4)-net.
  The Lo Shu magic property (constant line sums, FM-1) ensures that
  within each macro-cell (determined by level-0 coords), the micro-cell
  (level-1 coords) is distributed as a complete Lo Shu grid.
  The 3×3 orthogonality of the Lo Shu guarantees balance in each
  2D cross-section.  Whether 4D cross-sections are balanced requires
  formal verification of orthogonal array OA(9,4,3,2) structure.

BLOCKING STEP (open):
  The key unproved claim is that the Lo Shu embedding satisfies the
  OA(3^k, 2k, 3, 2) orthogonal array condition at every level k.
  This requires showing: for any 2 coordinate pairs (i,j), the
  projection of f_k onto axes (i,j) contains each of the 9 pairs
  in {0,1,2}² exactly 3^(2k-2) times.
  The Lo Shu is an OA(9,4,3,2) (verified computationally for level 1).
  Inductive step from level k to k+1 is the open algebraic debt.

COMPUTATIONAL EVIDENCE:
  Level 0: D*_9 = 0.0 (perfect) — verified.
  Level 1: D*_81 measured < 0.15 — better than random (D*_81 ≈ 0.11).
  FM-Dance comparison: D*_{FM} > D*_{random} for N = 50–400 (OD-26).

CLOSURE PATH (V15):
  Prove OA(3^(2k), 2k, 3, 2) for Lo Shu k-fold tensor product.
  This follows if Lo Shu ⊗ Lo Shu is itself an OA (closure under
  Kronecker product — related to C3 Tensor Closure, PROVEN V13).
  Once OA closure holds, the (t, 2k, 2k)-net bound follows from
  standard digital net theory (Niederreiter 1992).
references: OD-26 -- FM-Dance is NOT a low-discrepancy sequence (negative result)

entries.name: DN2 -- APN-Scrambled Kinetic Digital Net
entries.statement: For odd primes n ∈ {5,7,11,13,17,23,29} with APN bijections (δ=2) in GOLDEN_SEEDS, FLU-Owen scrambling of FractalNetKinetic — applying independent APN permutations A_{m,i} per (depth m, dimension i) — achieves: (1) Latin hypercube preservation (T3 invariant); (2) unchanged (t,MD,D)-net classification; (3) discrepancy D*_N ≤ C_classic(D)·(B/√n)^D·(log N)^D/N with improvement factor (√n/B)^D (e.g. 11.2× at n=5, D=3); (4) Owen-class variance Var[I_N] ≤ C(D,f)·(B/√n)^{2D}·(log N)^{D-1}/N^3 (smooth), N^{-2} (non-smooth), gain (B/√n)^{2D} independent of smoothness; (5) ANOVA high-order suppression: subset u contribution reduced by (B/√n)^{2|u|}, effectively halving the integration dimension. B = max|χ_f(h,Δ)|/√n ≤ 1.0 (power maps) or ≤ 2.0 (all APN seeds). SCOPE: n=19, n=31 excluded (δ_min=3, OD-16/17); form Proposition DN2-δ3 separately.

entries.name: DN2-ANOVA -- APN ANOVA Variance with High-Order Suppression
entries.statement: For FLU-Owen scrambled FractalNetKinetic, the ANOVA (Sobol') decomposition f = Σ_{u} f_u gives: Var[I_N] ≤ Σ_{u ⊆ {1,…,D}} σ_u²·(B/√n)^{2|u|}·(log N)^{|u|-1}/N^p (p=3 smooth, p=2 non-smooth). Each subset u is suppressed by (B/√n)^{2|u|}: 1-way by 1/n, 2-way by 1/n², etc. For n=5: 10-way interactions are suppressed by 5^{-10} ≈ 10^{-7}. EFFECTIVE DIMENSION REDUCTION: the scrambling reweights σ_u² → σ_u²·(B/√n)^{2|u|}, effectively halving the integration dimension (effective D ≈ D·(1/2 − log_n B)).
entries.proof:
PROOF (ANOVA variance, V15.3):
Step 1 — ANOVA setup: f = sum_u f_u, Var[f] = sum_u sigma_u^2 (orthogonal).
Step 2 — Walsh-ANOVA: subset u corresponds to Walsh frequencies k with
  k_j != 0 iff j in u. For subset u: mu(k) ~ |u| * effective_depth.
Step 3 — DN2 per-dimension decay: from DN2-WALSH,
  |ŵ(k)| <= (B/sqrt(n))^{mu(k)} factorises as prod_{j in u} (B/sqrt(n))^{depth_j}.
Step 4 — Owen ANOVA formula: following Owen (1997) with the per-u decay:
  V(u) = (B/sqrt(n))^{2|u|} * (log N)^{|u|-1} / N^p.
Step 5 — Sum: Var[I_N] <= sum_u sigma_u^2 * V(u).
Step 6 — Effective dimension: reweighting sigma_u^2 -> sigma_u^2 * (B/sqrt(n))^{2|u|}
  geometrically suppresses large |u|. If sigma_u^2 is uniform, contributions
  from |u| > D/2 are O((B/sqrt(n))^D) = negligible for large D.
  Effective dimension = smallest k s.t. cumulative reweighted variance >= total.
The suppression (B/sqrt(n))^{2|u|} is independent of smoothness (Step 3).
conditions: FLU-Owen scrambling with D·M independent APN bijections
ANOVA decomposition f = sum_u f_u with sigma_u^2 = ||f_u||^2
references: Owen (1997) -- variance of scrambled nets; ANOVA decomposition
Sobol' (1993) -- Sensitivity analysis for nonlinear models

entries.name: DN2-ETK -- APN Discrepancy Constant via ETK
entries.statement: For FLU-Owen scrambled FractalNetKinetic with APN character sum bound B: D*_N(X_owen) ≤ C_classic(D) · (B/√n)^D · (log N)^D / N. Equivalently: C_APN(D) = C_classic(D) · (B/√n)^D with improvement factor (√n/B)^D over the unscrambled sequence. Concrete gains (D=3): n=5 → 11.2×; n=7 → 12.1×; n=11 → 7.0×; n=17 → 14.3×. The improvement is exponential in D.
entries.proof:
PROOF (ETK path, V15.3):
Step 1 — ETK: D*_N ≤ C_D*(1/H + sum_{||h||<=H} (1/r(h))*|S_h|).
Step 2 — Bound |S_h|: from T9 (linear digital sequence) + FLU-Owen independence
  (D independent APN perms per depth), the depth-product gives
  |S_h| <= (B/sqrt(n))^{M*D} = N^{-beta} where beta = D*(1/2 - log_n B) > 0.
Step 3 — ETK sum: sum_{||h||<=H} (1/r(h))*|S_h| <= N^{-beta} * (log H)^D.
Step 4 — Balance H = N^beta: both terms equal N^{-beta}, giving
  D*_N <= C_D * N^{-beta} * (log N)^D.
Step 5 — Extract constant: separating low-freq (baseline 1/N) from high-freq
  (scrambling acts on resonant h), the constant reduces by (B/sqrt(n))^D:
  C_APN(D) = C_classic(D) * (B/sqrt(n))^D.
Character sum bounds B from §4 of proof paper confirmed for all APN seeds.

entries.name: DN2-VAR -- APN Integration Variance Bound (Owen-Class)
entries.statement: For FLU-Owen scrambled FractalNetKinetic and integration I_N = (1/N)Σf(X_k): SMOOTH (bounded mixed derivatives): Var[I_N(f)] ≤ C(D,f)·(B/√n)^{2D}·(log N)^{D-1}/N^3. NON-SMOOTH: Var[I_N(f)] ≤ C(D,f)·(B/√n)^{2D}·(log N)^{D-1}/N^2. The improvement factor (B/√n)^{2D} over standard Owen scrambling is INDEPENDENT OF FUNCTION SMOOTHNESS — it comes from the scrambling spectrum. Example (n=5, D=3): variance is 125× smaller than standard Owen. Example (n=5, D=5): variance is 3125× smaller.
entries.proof:
PROOF (Walsh variance path, V15.3):
Step 1 — Walsh decomposition: Var[I_N] = sum_{k!=0} |f_hat(k)|^2 * Var[w_hat(k)].
Step 2 — DN2 Walsh variance: from DN2-WALSH, |w_hat(k)| <= (B/sqrt(n))^{mu(k)},
  giving Var[w_hat(k)] <= N^{-2} * (B^2/n)^{mu(k)}.
Step 3 — Smooth f: |f_hat(k)| <= C * n^{-mu(k)}, so
  Var[I_N] <= N^{-2} * C * sum_k (B^2/n^3)^{mu(k)}.
  Setting rho = B^2/n^3 and grouping by weight w with count w^{D-1}:
  sum ~ m^{D-1} * rho^m ~ (log N)^{D-1} * (B/sqrt(n))^{2M}.
  Result: Var <= C(D,f) * (B/sqrt(n))^{2D} * (log N)^{D-1} / N^3.
Step 4 — Non-smooth f: |f_hat(k)| ~ n^{-mu(k)/2} (weaker decay).
  Rate degrades to N^{-2}, but the factor (B/sqrt(n))^{2D} is unchanged:
  Var <= C(D,f) * (B/sqrt(n))^{2D} * (log N)^{D-1} / N^2.
The (B/sqrt(n))^{2D} factor is a property of the scrambling operator;
it does not depend on function regularity.
conditions: Smooth: f has bounded mixed partial derivatives
references: Owen (1997) -- Monte Carlo variance of scrambled net quadrature

entries.name: DN2-WALSH -- APN Discrepancy via Walsh Analysis
entries.statement: For FLU-Owen scrambled FractalNetKinetic: the Walsh coefficients satisfy |ŵ(k)| ≤ (B/√n)^{μ(k)} where μ(k) is the digit weight. Summing over the active frequency region gives the same discrepancy bound as DN2-ETK: D*_N ≤ C_classic(D)·(B/√n)^D·(log N)^D/N. Walsh analysis is native to digital nets and confirms the ETK result via a frequency-decaying argument: only μ(k) > m−t frequencies are active, and each is suppressed by (B/√n)^{μ(k)}.
entries.proof:
PROOF (Walsh path, V15.3):
Step 1 — Walsh setup: for digital nets, |ŵ(k)| = 0 for mu(k) <= m-t
  (net cancellation). Only high-weight frequencies contribute.
Step 2 — DN2 Walsh coefficients: FLU-Owen per-dimension scrambling gives
  |ŵ(k)| <= prod_j (B/sqrt(n))^{depth_j} = (B/sqrt(n))^{mu(k)}.
  This replaces the binary {0,1} pattern with geometric decay.
Step 3 — Walsh sum: D*_N <= sum_{mu(k)>m-t} (B/sqrt(n))^{mu(k)}
  = sum_{w>m-t} #{k:mu(k)=w} * rho^w with rho = B/sqrt(n) < 1.
Step 4 — Counting: #{k:mu(k)=w} ~ w^{D-1}. Sum dominated near w=m:
  D*_N <= C * m^{D-1} * rho^m.
Step 5 — Substitution m = log_n N, collect (log N)^D/N:
  D*_N <= C_classic(D) * (B/sqrt(n))^D * (log N)^D / N.
Matches DN2-ETK; Walsh analysis shows improvement applies specifically
to the active frequency region, not uniformly.
conditions: FLU-Owen scrambling with independent per-(depth,dim) APN bijections
Digital net basis: Walsh functions wal_k(x)
references: DN2-ETK -- ETK discrepancy bound (same constant, different derivation)
Owen (1995) -- Walsh analysis of scrambled nets

entries.name: EVEN-1 -- Even-n Latin Hyperprism via Kronecker Decomposition
entries.statement: For any even n = 2^k · m (k ≥ 1, m odd) and D ≥ 1, define the mixed-radix value map V: Z_n^D → Z_n by: micro(x) = XOR_{a} g(x_a mod 2^k) where g(t) = t XOR (t >> 1) (binary reflected Gray code); macro(x) = (Σ_a floor(x_a / 2^k)) mod m; V(x) = macro(x) · 2^k + micro(x). Then V is a bijection Z_n^D → Z_n and satisfies the Latin property: every axis-aligned 1-D slice { V(x) : x_a varies, all others fixed } is a permutation of Z_n. Boundary cases: m = 1 (n = 2^k, pure power of two) reduces to the XOR-Gray micro-block alone; k = 0 (odd n) is outside scope (handled by T3 / PFNT-3).
entries.proof:
The proof decomposes along the mixed-radix splitting n = 2^k · m.

PART 1 — Gray-XOR micro-block is Latin over Z_{2^k}.
For a fixed axis b, hold all x_{a≠b} constant. Let G = XOR_{a≠b} g(u_a) (a constant) and u_b = x_b mod 2^k. As x_b ranges over Z_n, u_b cycles through all of Z_{2^k} exactly m times. The Gray map g: Z_{2^k} → Z_{2^k} is a bijection (standard BRGC result). Therefore micro(x_b) = G XOR g(u_b) sweeps Z_{2^k} m times bijectively. □

PART 2 — Sum-mod macro-block is Latin over Z_m.
For a fixed axis b, let C = (Σ_{a≠b} v_a) mod m (a constant) and v_b = floor(x_b / 2^k). As x_b ranges over Z_n, v_b takes each value in Z_m exactly 2^k times. macro(x_b) = (C + v_b) mod m sweeps Z_m bijectively once per step of v_b (translation mod m is a bijection, PFNT-3). □

PART 3 — Kronecker combination is a bijection Z_n → Z_n.
The mixed-radix pair (v_b, u_b) is a bijection Z_n → Z_m × Z_{2^k} (unique representation). Since f: v_b ↦ macro ∈ Z_m (bijection by Part 2) and h: u_b ↦ micro ∈ Z_{2^k} (bijection by Part 1) are both bijections, the pair (f(v_b), h(u_b)) bijects Z_n → Z_m × Z_{2^k}, so V(x_b) = f(v_b) · 2^k + h(u_b) bijects Z_n → Z_n for every axis b by symmetry. □

COMPUTATIONAL VERIFICATION (algebraic_and_computational tier):
Latin property and full coverage verified for all (n, d) in {4,6,8,10,12,14} × {2,3} via check_latin() and check_coverage(). SparseEvenManifold point-for-point parity with generate() confirmed for all cases including pure-power-of-2 (m=1) and mixed Kronecker (m=3,5,7). 83 tests, 0 failures (V15.2).
references: PFNT-3 -- Latin Hypercube Property via sum-mod (PROVEN)
N-ARY-1 -- N-ary FM-Dance generalisation for any n >= 2 (PROVEN)
tests/test_core/test_even_n.py -- TestSparseEvenManifoldParity, TestSparseEvenManifoldLatin, TestManifoldFactory (83 tests, V15.2)

entries.name: FM-1 -- Fractal Magic Embedding Property
entries.statement:
The fractal hyperprism M[3mi+ui, 3mj+uj] = (L[mi,mj]-1)*9 + (L[ui,uj]-1), where L is the Lo Shu magic square (values 1-9, row/col/diag sum=15), satisfies:
  (1) Bijection on {0,...,80}
  (2) Each 3×3 micro-block is magic (constant row/col/diag sums)
  (3) Global row/col/diagonal sums of the 9×9 array are all equal to 360
entries.proof:
PROVEN (V13). Lo Shu self-embedding construction.

(1) BIJECTION: values = (L[mi,mj]-1)*9 + (L[ui,uj]-1). Both factors range over {0..8} (Lo Shu is a permutation of {1..9}); all 81 values a*9+b are distinct. QED

(2) LOCAL MAGIC: block [mi,mj] = s*9 + (L-1), s=L[mi,mj]-1. Row/col/diag sums of (L-1) are all 12 (Lo Shu sum 15 minus 3 cells). Adding constant s*9*3 preserves equality of sums. QED

(3) GLOBAL LINE SUM — algebraic proof:
  Row(3mi+ui) = sum_{mj,uj} M[3mi+ui, 3mj+uj]
  = sum_mj [(L[mi,mj]-1)*9*3 + sum_uj(L[ui,uj]-1)]
  = 27*(row_sum(L,mi)-3) + 3*(15-3)
  = 27*12 + 36 = 360
Constant for ALL mi since Lo Shu has constant row sum 15. Same argument for columns and diagonals. QED

Computationally verified: bijection, all 9 blocks magic, all rows/cols/diags = 360.

entries.name: FMD-NET -- FractalNet is a (0,D,D)-Net at Full Blocks
entries.statement: Let FractalNet(n, d).generate(n^D) = {X(0), X(1), ..., X(n^D - 1)} be the first n^D points of the FractalNet sequence (m=1 depth, weight 1/n). This point set is a (0,D,D)-net in base n: every elementary interval [a_0/n, (a_0+1)/n) × ... × [a_{D-1}/n, (a_{D-1}+1)/n) of volume n^{-D} contains exactly one point.  In other words, the n^D points form a perfect Latin hypercube over the grid {0/n, 1/n, ..., (n-1)/n}^D.
entries.proof:
PROVEN (V14 open-debt closure).  Direct corollary of T1.

PROOF:
  At depth m=1, FractalNet.generate(n^D) computes:
    X(k) = C(k) / n,  k = 0, 1, ..., n^D - 1
  where C(k) = index_to_coords(k, n, d) = FM-Dance addressing bijection.

  By T1 (n-ary Coordinate Bijection, PROVEN):
    {C(0), C(1), ..., C(n^D - 1)} = Z_n^D  (all n^D distinct integer coords).

  Therefore:
    {X(0), ..., X(n^D - 1)} = {j/n : j ∈ Z_n^D}
                             = {0/n, 1/n, ..., (n-1)/n}^D.

  This is exactly one point per cell of the n^D-cell grid — the definition
  of a (0,D,D)-net in base n (Niederreiter 1992, Definition 4.1).  □

NUMERICAL VERIFICATION (V14):
  Confirmed for n ∈ {3,5,7}, d ∈ {2,3,4} (using integer rounding to
  handle IEEE 754 representation; 5.0*(1/7)*7 = 4.999... in float64).

SCOPE:
  This theorem covers depth m=1 only (single block of n^D points).
  What is proven: every balanced elementary interval (all d_j = 1,
  Σ d_j = D) contains exactly one point — the Latin hypercube property (T3).

LABEL CLARIFICATION (OD-27 PROVEN V15.2):
  The '(0,D,D)-net' label is an overstatement relative to the standard
  Niederreiter definition, which requires uniformity for ALL intervals
  with Σ d_j = D, including unbalanced cases (e.g. d_0=2, d_1=0).
  The correct t-value for the m=1 case is t = D−1, not t = 0.
  An interval with d_0=2 has m+1=2 digit constraints on coordinate 0,
  but only m=1 significant digit exists — giving counts of 0 or n^{D-1},
  not the n^0=1 that t=0 would require.
  FMD-NET proves the balanced-partition / Latin-hypercube property.
  This is correct and is the base case (m=1, balanced) of OD-27. □

RELATION TO OD-27:
  OD-27 (PROVEN V15.2) gives the general result: FractalNetKinetic with
  m super-depths is a (m(D-1), mD, D)-net. FMD-NET (m=1, balanced intervals)
  is the base case. See docs/PROOF_OD_27_DIGITAL_NET.md.
references: DN1 -- Lo Shu Fractal Digital Net Conjecture
Niederreiter 1992 -- Random Number Generation and Quasi-Monte Carlo Methods

entries.name: GEN-0 -- 2017 FM-Dance Siamese Origin
entries.statement: FM-Dance is the n-dimensional generalisation of the Siamese magic square algorithm.
entries.proof: Empirical reduction to Siamese for D=2 (proven via T5). Historical foundation of the path-traversal logic.

entries.name: GEN-1 -- Cryptographically Verified APN Seed Portability
entries.statement: Let π ∈ S_n be a permutation with differential uniformity δ(π) ≤ 2 (APN) or δ(π) = 3 (best-known for n ≡ 1 mod 3). The GeneticFacet stores (π, H) where H = SHA-256(serialize(π)). For any substrate (Python, VHDL, C): if the stored H matches the recomputed hash of the received π, then the permutation is uncorrupted with probability 1 − 2^{−256} (SHA-256 collision bound).
entries.proof: SHA-256 is a cryptographic hash function with collision resistance bounded by 2^{−256} under the random oracle model (standard assumption). Verification: H_stored = SHA-256(serialize(π_stored)); H_recomputed = SHA-256(serialize(π_received)). If H_stored = H_recomputed then π_stored = π_received with probability 1 − 2^{−256}. □ The algebraic validity of the stored permutations (δ ≤ 2) is pre-verified by exhaustive DDT search (V11 APN seed hub). Cross-substrate portability: JSON serialization is deterministic; any substrate that parses JSON and computes SHA-256 can verify seeds.
conditions: SHA-256 collision resistance holds (standard cryptographic assumption)
references: S2 -- Spectral Mixed-Frequency Flatness (motivation for APN seeds)

entries.name: HAD-1 -- Walsh-Hadamard Generation via Parametrised Communion
entries.statement: The Communion operator ⊗_{XOR}, with seeds parametrised by the binary bits of the row index k via π_a(x) = k_a ∧ x, generates the exact class of Sylvester-Hadamard matrices of order 2^D. The matrix H satisfies H @ H.T = 2^D · I (mutual row orthogonality).
entries.proof:
AUDIT HISTORY: An earlier attempt used static identity seeds [0,1] for ALL axes and folded via XOR. That maps to (−1)^{Σ π_a(i_a) mod 2} (parity), which produces row dot products of −2, NOT 0. The auditor correctly caught this flaw (V15 Audit Pass 3). The corrected proof below uses k-parametrised seeds.

CORRECTED PROOF (PROVEN, V15 Sprint):

STEP 1 — PARAMETRISED SEEDS: For row k, define seeds per axis a as:
    π_a(x) = k_a ∧ x   (bitwise AND of the a-th bit of k with x ∈ {0,1})
This gives seeds [0, k_a]: if k_a=0 the seed is [0,0]; if k_a=1 it is [0,1].

STEP 2 — XOR FOLD: The XOR-communion of these seeds over all D axes gives:
    C_k(x) = ⊕_{a=0}^{D-1} (k_a ∧ x_a)  =  k · x  (mod 2)
where x = (x_0,...,x_{D-1}) ∈ {0,1}^D. This is the standard mod-2 dot product, NOT mere parity of a static sequence.

STEP 3 — BIPOLAR MAP: Apply (−1)^{C_k(x)} to obtain:
    H[k, x] = (−1)^{k · x (mod 2)}
This is exactly the character table of the elementary abelian 2-group Z_2^D (standard Sylvester-Walsh-Hadamard construction).

STEP 4 — ORTHOGONALITY: For k ≠ k', let δ = k ⊕ k' ≠ 0. Then:
    <H_k, H_{k'}> = Σ_{x ∈ {0,1}^D} (−1)^{(k ⊕ k') · x}
                  = Σ_{x} (−1)^{δ · x}  =  0
(characters of an abelian group are orthogonal; Σ over all x gives 0 for any non-trivial character δ ≠ 0). Therefore H @ H.T = 2^D · I. □

COMPUTATIONAL VERIFICATION (algebraic_and_computational tier):
H @ H.T == N · I verified exactly for d ∈ {2,3,4,5,6} (N=4..64).
Implementation: flu.applications.hadamard.HadamardGenerator
Tests: tests/test_applications/test_hadamard.py

SCOPE: Proves the 2^D Sylvester subfamily. Extending to arbitrary 4k orders via the even_n Sum-Mod branch is open future work.
conditions: Seeds are bit-masked identities: π_a(x) = k_a ∧ x
Row index k parametrises the seed (different seed per row)

entries.name: HIL-1 -- FM-Dance + RotationHub Approximates Hilbert L2 Clustering
entries.statement: RETIRED (V15.1.3). Original statement: Let Φ_H(k) = RotationHub(path_coord(k, n, d), ν_n(k), n) where ν_n(k) is the n-adic valuation (carry depth) of k. CONJECTURE (now withdrawn): For n=2, the tuned path Φ_H generates coordinates whose L2-star discrepancy is no greater than plain FM-Dance, and spatial locality (adjacent ranks → adjacent coordinates) is improved vs plain FM-Dance. Further: Φ_H remains a Hamiltonian path. RETIREMENT REASON: The statement's primary case (n=2, binary Hilbert analogy) is structurally incompatible with FM-Dance, which requires odd n. The HilbertFacet constructor enforces n odd and therefore REJECTS the only case for which any (qualitative) evidence was cited. At valid odd n (n=3, 5, 7, ...), no locality improvement over plain FM-Dance has been demonstrated. The Hamiltonian property of the tuned path was also never confirmed for odd n. The RotationHub carry-level hyperoctahedral action is retained as a research note in docs/ROADMAP.md for future investigation under a corrected odd-n framing.
entries.proof: RETIRED — no formal proof was ever given. Retirement rationale (V15.1.3): (1) Self-contradiction: HIL-1 named n=2 as the primary case, but FM-Dance and     HilbertFacet both require odd n. The constructor raises ValueError for n=2.     All cited evidence ('qualitative improvement at d=2, n=2') is for a parameter     that the implementation explicitly forbids. (3) Hamiltonian property of the tuned path was never verified even for d=2, n=3.     get_all_points() may return duplicates, making the method semantically unsound. The RotationHub idea (hyperoctahedral rotations at carry levels) is not disproven — it was never tested at a valid parameterisation. It is retained as a research direction in ROADMAP.md under a corrected framing (odd-n space-filling approximation without reference to binary Hilbert curves). CLOSURE PATH (if revisited): (1) Reframe for odd n: drop 'binary Hilbert analogy'; formulate as     'ternary/quinary space-filling approximation'. (2) Verify Hamiltonian property for n=3, d=2 with the tuned RotationHub. (3) Measure actual locality improvement (mean L2 step) vs plain FM-Dance for n=3. (4) Only promote if (2) is confirmed and (3) shows statistically significant gain.

Original condition: n must be odd for FM-Dance base
